title: Masha A
text:
Uninsured Rates for Adults Ages 19-64 by Sex
Your exploratory data analysis of the team datasets go here.
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(gtsummary)
library(visdat)

uninsured_data <- read_csv("../data/raw/uninsured_sex_2024.csv", skip = 2) |> 
  clean_names()

title: Masha A
section: Is there a gender gap in uninsurance rates?
text:
Is there a gender gap in uninsurance rates?


Code
uninsured_long <- uninsured_data |>
  filter(location != "United States") |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = c(male, female), names_to = "sex", values_to = "rate")

ggplot(uninsured_long, aes(x = rate, fill = sex)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Uninsured Rates by Sex Across States",
       x = "Uninsured Rate (%)",
       y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Masha A
text:
Geographic Variation


Code
uninsured_data |>
  filter(location != "United States") |>
  mutate(gap = male - female) |>
  slice_max(gap, n = 10) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(location, gap), y = gap)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 States with Largest Gender Gap",
       subtitle = "States where men are significantly more likely to be uninsured than women",
       x = "State",
       y = "Difference (Male % - Female %)") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Masha A
text:
Initial EDA Observations
Gender Disparity - Initial density plots suggest that males generally have a higher uninsurance rate than females in the 19-64 age bracket. This may be linked to female eligibility for programs like Medicaid during pregnancy or different employment patterns
State Volatility - There is a range in rates. States that did not expand Medicaid likely show higher total uninsured percentages for both sexes compared to expansion states

title: Olivia S
text:
Data Cleaning
Code
raw_coverage <- read_csv("../data/raw/Heathcare_Coverage_US.csv")

df <- raw_coverage %>%
  select(-Total) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Location), !str_starts(Location, "Notes")) %>%
  drop_na(Employer) %>%
  rename_with(~ c("location", "employer", "non_group", "medicaid",
                  "medicare", "military", "uninsured"), everything())

# Separate national from state-level (territories)
national <- df |> filter(location == "United States")
states   <- df |> filter(location != "United States")
Code
glimpse(states)


Rows: 52
Columns: 7
$ location  <chr> "Alabama", "Alaska", "Arizona", "Arkansas", "California", "C…
$ employer  <dbl> 0.470, 0.462, 0.467, 0.422, 0.470, 0.518, 0.517, 0.485, 0.55…
$ non_group <dbl> 0.068, 0.047, 0.058, 0.060, 0.064, 0.077, 0.049, 0.050, 0.04…

title: Olivia S
text:
Summary Statistics


Code
stats_tbl <- states %>%
  select(-location) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "coverage_type", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(coverage_type) %>%
  summarise(Mean   = mean(value),
    Median = median(value),
    SD     = sd(value),
    Min    = min(value),
    Max    = max(value),
    .groups = "drop") %>% 
  mutate(coverage_type = str_to_title(str_replace_all(coverage_type, "_", " ")),
    across(where(is.numeric), ~ percent(.x, accuracy = 0.1))) %>%
  arrange(desc(Mean)) %>%
  rename("Coverage Type" = coverage_type)

knitr::kable(stats_tbl, align = "lrrrrr",
             caption = "Summary statistics by coverage type (states & territories)")

Key observations:

Employer coverage is by far the largest category, averaging 48.7% across states.
Uninsured rates have the widest spread (SD = 2.6%), reflecting large policy differences across states.
Military coverage is the smallest category on average at 1.5%.

title: Olivia S
text:
Visualisations

Coverage Rates by Type


Code
states %>%
  pivot_longer(-location, names_to = "type", values_to = "pct") %>%
  mutate(type = str_to_title(str_replace_all(type, "_", " "))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = pct, fill = type)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  facet_wrap(~ type, scales = "free", ncol = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title    = "Distribution of Coverage Rates by Type",
    subtitle = "Histogram of state/territory proportions for each coverage category",
    x = "Share of Population", y = "Count") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")












Top States: Uninsured vs. Employer Coverage


Code
make_ranked_plot <- function(col, n = 10, title, fill_col) {
  states |>
    select(location, value = {{ col }}) |>
    slice_max(value, n = n) |>
    mutate(location = fct_reorder(location, value)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = value, y = location)) +
    geom_col(fill = fill_col, alpha = 0.85) +
    geom_text(aes(label = percent(value, accuracy = 0.1)),
              hjust = -0.1, size = 3.2) +
    scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                       expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
    labs(title = title, x = NULL, y = NULL)
}

p3a <- make_ranked_plot(uninsured, 10, "Top 10: Highest Uninsured Rate",   "#E74C3C")
p3b <- make_ranked_plot(employer,  10, "Top 10: Highest Employer Coverage", "#2980B9")

p3a | p3b





Top 10 states ranked by uninsured rate (left) and employer coverage (right)







Correlation Between Coverage Types


Code
cor_mat <- states %>%
  select(-location) %>%
  cor() %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("var1") %>%
  pivot_longer(-var1, names_to = "var2", values_to = "corr") %>%
  mutate(
    var1 = str_to_title(str_replace_all(var1, "_", " ")),
    var2 = str_to_title(str_replace_all(var2, "_", " "))
  )

ggplot(cor_mat, aes(x = var1, y = var2, fill = corr)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(corr, 2)), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#E74C3C", mid = "white", high = "#2980B9",
                       midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1),
                       name = "Pearson r") +
  labs(
    title    = "Correlation Matrix: Coverage Types",
    subtitle = "Computed across all states and territories",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))









Notable correlations:

Employer ↔︎ Medicaid: strong negative (-0.75) — states with higher employer coverage tend to have lower Medicaid enrollment.
Medicaid ↔︎ Uninsured: negative (-0.21) — expanded Medicaid is associated with fewer uninsured residents.




Medicaid vs. Uninsured Rate


Code
states %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = medicaid, y = uninsured, label = location)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = employer), size = 3, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "grey30",
              linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 2.6, max.overlaps = 20, color = "grey30") +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "yellow", high = "red",
                       labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
                       name   = "Employer\nCoverage") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Medicaid Enrollment vs. Uninsured Rate by State",
    subtitle = "Color = employer coverage share; dashed line = linear trend",
    x = "Medicaid Share", y = "Uninsured Rate"
  )

title: Olivia S
text:
Findings



Metric
States




Highest uninsured rates
Texas, Georgia, Oklahoma


Lowest uninsured rates
Massachusetts, Hawaii, Vermont


Highest Medicaid share
Puerto Rico, New Mexico, Louisiana


Highest employer share
Utah, New Hampshire, District of Columbia




Nationally, 48.6% of the population relies on employer-sponsored insurance.
8.2% of the population remains uninsured, with wide state variation.
Medicaid expansion is the single strongest predictor of lower uninsured rates across states.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Title: “Investigating The Health Insurance Coverage of the US Population Across Poverty Level” Olivia and Masha
Description: This project will look at the health insurance status of the US population by state. We will explore the realtionships between status (below the poverty line, in the millitary, gender…etc) and heathcare coverage (medicaid, uninsured, insured by employer…etc).
research questions: How strong is the correlation between poverty level and medicare coverage? Are there groups more likely to be uninsured? Does race/ethnicity have an impact on healthcare coverage?
the reasons/inspirations behind choosing the topic: We are both interested in healthcare disparities within the US and especially how people are affected witht the new cuts to social prgrams such as medicare/medicaid.
one potential data set that can be used to answer the research questions: There are multiple at this link: https://www.kff.org/state-category/health-coverage-uninsured/ and also on the US census website
communication plan: we will communicate through email, and the expected response time will be 24 hours. Right now, meeting times will be 10:45 on wednesday, but subject to change.
roles: Masha-point of contact and style lead, Olivia-technical lead
conflict resolution: we will have clear communication, if the deadline is missed, it will be made up, and if the team is split on something, the team lead responsible for the area of conflict will take the lead (ex: on a technical disagreement, Olivia is the lead and we will do her way.)
a rough implementation plan: see github (this section to be edited once we have more specific tasks)

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text: “The Differences in How CNN, MSNBC, and FOX Cover the News” (The Pudding, 2018) Summary This report by The Pudding does a good job at telling a data story; it avoids overwhelming the reader by breaking down the data and visualizations in annotated chunks. It unpacks the data in layers, building upon understanding of the previous visualization to inform the next one. The authors are trying to get the reader to understand how this kind of bias works through data; but to be convincing they have to take it one step at a time. The story starts by establishing the different networks, and how they could have different word usages (ex: stations say the names of their reporters the most often). These structured examples provide intuition on the visualization itself, and help the reader understand what the placement of the actual data points mean. The next step is to provide case studies within the visualization (ex: who talked the most about natural disasters). Once the reader has understood how different networks could provide biases, the story moves one step further; while stations may use the same words, it does not mean that they are telling the same story. The report then introduces contextual framing by analyzing words commonly paired with others (ex: all networks say “gun,” but Fox pairs it with “control,” CNN/MSNBC pair it with “violence”). Each ‘section’ of the report has an open ended interactive section for the reader, to test their understanding, and apply what they have just learned to topics not explicitly mentioned in the report. One possible limitation may be that the report relies heavily on interactive elements, while this makes it engaging, it may be harder to fully understand if the reader does not actively explore the visualizations.
